content.text: Streaming lets Claude's response appear token by token rather than arriving all at once after a multi-second wait. In a mobile chat UI, this transforms the experience — users start reading immediately and never stare at a blank screen. This document covers every layer of the streaming stack in React Native: the polyfill required to make
API, state management patterns, cancellation, and the performance trade-offs to be aware of.

attrs.alt: Comparison: non-streaming waits then shows full text; streaming renders tokens incrementally
attrs.title: Streaming vs non-streaming response experience in React Native

content.text: Why Streaming Needs Extra Setup on React Native
The Anthropic SDK's streaming is built on the Fetch
API — specifically the async iterable protocol (
). Hermes, the JavaScript engine React Native uses, shipped a partial
implementation that does not support this protocol. Without a polyfill, calling
TypeError: stream[Symbol.asyncIterator] is not a function
The fix is to replace the global
implementation before any SDK code runs.
Apply the Polyfill at the Entry Point
The polyfill must run before any import that touches the SDK. In Expo, that means

reference and streaming will fail. Keep the polyfill as the absolute first executable statement in the entry file.

to accumulate streamed text without race conditions. Each chunk update is queued as a function of the previous state:
React Native may re-render on every
call during the stream, which can cause jank if the component tree is expensive to render. Accumulating chunks in a ref and flushing every N milliseconds reduces render pressure:
Users tap a Stop button; navigation unmounts the component mid-stream. Both cases require cancellation. Pass an
Putting it together — a chat screen with streaming output, a Stop button, and automatic scroll to the bottom:
Instead of the async iterable loop, you can use the event emitter interface that
also exposes. This is useful when you want to handle different event types separately or integrate with an existing event-driven architecture:
Slow layout as text grows long
Memoize client with useMemo or module-level singleton
Always use functional setState: prev => prev + chunk

Expecting streaming through a non-streaming proxy.
If your backend proxy buffers the full response before forwarding (e.g., the default Express
) the client will not see incremental chunks regardless of the SDK setting. The proxy must use chunked Transfer-Encoding and flush each delta immediately.
